verses.text: Job grants the truth but asks, 'how should man be just with God?' — none could answer Him once in a thousand challenges. He feels the infinite distance between God and man. The question is the deepest of all, and its answer waits for the righteousness God Himself provides.

He extols the God who removes mountains, shakes the earth from its place, and commands the sun not to rise. Creation trembles at His word. Such power leaves Job awed and small, unable to dispute with his Maker.

He extols the God who removes mountains, shakes the earth from its place, and commands the sun not to rise. Creation trembles at His word. Such power leaves Job awed and small, unable to dispute with his Maker.

He extols the God who removes mountains, shakes the earth from its place, and commands the sun not to rise. Creation trembles at His word. Such power leaves Job awed and small, unable to dispute with his Maker.

God alone spreads out the heavens and makes Arcturus, Orion, and the Pleiades, doing great things 'past finding out.' The starry skies declare His unsearchable wisdom. The believer who ponders the heavens learns humility before their Maker.

God alone spreads out the heavens and makes Arcturus, Orion, and the Pleiades, doing great things 'past finding out.' The starry skies declare His unsearchable wisdom. The believer who ponders the heavens learns humility before their Maker.

'He goeth by me, and I see him not,' Job says; who dares ask, 'what doest thou?' He feels God's nearness yet hiddenness. We cannot summon God to explain Himself, but must trust the wisdom we cannot trace.

'He goeth by me, and I see him not,' Job says; who dares ask, 'what doest thou?' He feels God's nearness yet hiddenness. We cannot summon God to explain Himself, but must trust the wisdom we cannot trace.

'He goeth by me, and I see him not,' Job says; who dares ask, 'what doest thou?' He feels God's nearness yet hiddenness. We cannot summon God to explain Himself, but must trust the wisdom we cannot trace.

How then, asks Job, can he answer such a God? Though innocent, he could only plead for mercy from his Judge. He sees that even the upright cannot stand on their own merits. Before God, our only hope is grace, not argument.

God, he feels, breaks him with a tempest and multiplies his wounds; though righteous, his own mouth would condemn him. He is overwhelmed by God's apparent severity. In such darkness, faith must hold to God's goodness against all that the senses say.

In bitterness Job blurts that God 'destroyeth the perfect and the wicked' alike, and the earth is given to the wicked. His grief overstates the case. Even good men, in pain, may speak rashly of God's ways, which are far higher than they can see.

His days flee swifter than a runner, fleeting as a ship or an eagle swooping; he dreads all his sorrows. Life races by under a cloud of fear. When joy is gone, even the speed of time becomes a torment.

Were he to wash in snow water and cleanse his hands, he feels God would still plunge him in the ditch till his own clothes abhorred him. He despairs of self-cleansing. No washing of our own can make us clean before God; we need a purity given from above.

God, says Job, is not a man that he could meet Him in court, 'neither is there any daysman betwixt us, that might lay his hand upon us both.' He longs for a mediator. That very longing is answered in Christ, the one Daysman who joins God and man.
